telemetry.logs_summary:
FATAL: dial tcp postgres:5432: connection refused
FATAL: max_connections exceeded on postgresql-primary
panic: runtime error: invalid memory address

rca.root_cause: Database connection pool exhaustion triggered by auth-service v2.4.1 deployment introducing connection leak in PostgreSQL pool initializer.
evidence: auth-service deployed at 10:00:00, crashes began 72 seconds later
PostgreSQL connection count hit 100/100 (max_connections) at 10:01:12
Crash logs show 'connection refused' on every startup attempt — service cannot acquire DB connection to initialize
No similar crashes in previous 14 days — deployment is the change event
remediation: Immediately rollback auth-service to v2.4.0
Increase PostgreSQL max_connections to 200 in postgresql.conf
Add connection pool limits to auth-service config (max_pool_size: 20)
Implement exponential backoff on DB connection retry
kubectl_commands: kubectl exec -it postgresql-primary-0 -n data -- psql -U postgres -c 'SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity;'
rca.summary: auth-service v2.4.1 deployment introduced a PostgreSQL connection leak causing pool exhaustion within 72 seconds, triggering CrashLoopBackOff across all auth pods and cascading authentication failures to 4 downstream services.

title: Redis Cluster Outage — Cache Layer Failure

telemetry.logs_summary:
FATAL: OOM command not allowed when used memory > 'maxmemory'
WARN: 1024 changes in 300 seconds

timeline.event: Redis memory at 95% — eviction policy kicking in
Redis OOMKilled — pod evicted by kubelet

rca.root_cause: Redis cluster OOMKilled due to unbounded memory growth from order-service session accumulation without TTL expiration configured.
evidence: Redis memory grew linearly from 8:00 AM — no expiration on order session keys
maxmemory policy set to noeviction — no keys were evicted before OOM
order-service deployed session caching feature 3 days ago without TTL
kubelet OOMKill event at 09:45:00
remediation: Set Redis maxmemory-policy to allkeys-lru
Add TTL to all order-service session keys (EXPIRE key 3600)
Increase Redis memory limit from 512Mi to 1Gi in deployment manifest
Add Redis memory alerting at 70% threshold
kubectl_commands: kubectl exec -it redis-cluster-0 -n data -- redis-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru
rca.summary: Redis cluster OOMKilled after order-service introduced unbounded session caching without TTL, causing 100% cache miss rate on auth sessions and cart data unavailability.

telemetry.logs_summary:
WARN: External payment gateway timeout after 5000ms
WARN: Retry attempt 3/3 failed
ERROR: Transaction processing queue depth: 847

timeline.event: External payment gateway latency increased to 3s

rca.root_cause: External payment gateway experiencing degraded performance causing cascading queue buildup in payment-service with no circuit breaker configured.
evidence: Gateway latency increased from baseline 200ms to 5000ms+ at 08:28
No circuit breaker — payment-service continues accepting requests while gateway is slow
Queue depth grew from 0 to 847 in 2 minutes
order-service has no timeout override — inherits payment-service delays
remediation: Implement circuit breaker pattern on external gateway calls
Set request timeout to 3s with fallback response
Add HPA scaling trigger on queue depth metric
Notify users of payment delays via notification-service
kubectl_commands: kubectl exec -it payment-service-9f2c1d-vp3r -n default -- curl -w '%{time_total}' payment-gateway/health
rca.summary: External payment gateway degradation caused payment-service queue buildup to 847 pending transactions. No circuit breaker allowed cascading timeout failures to order-service and frontend checkout flow.

telemetry.logs_summary:
FATAL: listen tcp :53: bind: address already in use
[ERROR] plugin/errors: 2 SERVFAIL

timeline.event: CoreDNS pod crashed — port 53 bind conflict
SRE team paged — full cluster impact
Stale CoreDNS pod identified and deleted
DNS resolution restored — all services recovering

rca.root_cause: CoreDNS pod failed to release port 53 during rolling update, causing new pod to fail binding — full cluster DNS outage for 13 minutes.
evidence: Port 53 bind conflict in CoreDNS logs
Stale pod from previous rollout still holding port
All services showing NXDOMAIN for internal service discovery
Node-level netstat confirmed two processes competing for :53
remediation: Delete stale CoreDNS pods during rollout
Add preStop hook to CoreDNS to gracefully release port 53
Implement DNS health check in readiness probe
Add CoreDNS PodDisruptionBudget
rca.summary: CoreDNS rolling update left a stale pod holding port 53, causing DNS resolution failure across the entire cluster for 13 minutes. All services were affected until stale pod deletion at 07:22.

telemetry.logs_summary:
AUDIT: ServiceAccount auth-sa bound to ClusterRole cluster-admin
AUDIT: Unexpected secret access: api-keys-secret from auth-pod

timeline.event: Attack path detected: auth-pod → cluster-admin → all secrets

rca.root_cause: auth-sa ServiceAccount was bound to cluster-admin ClusterRole, granting a pod-level service account unrestricted cluster access, enabling lateral movement to secrets outside its operational scope.
evidence: ClusterRoleBinding auth-sa-admin-binding created at 05:58
Audit logs show auth-sa accessing api-keys-secret — not in its required permissions
Principle of least privilege violated — auth-sa needs only read on postgres-credentials
No NetworkPolicy restricting auth-pod egress
remediation: Immediately delete ClusterRoleBinding auth-sa-admin-binding
Create scoped Role with only required permissions (read postgres-credentials)
Rotate postgres-credentials and api-keys-secret immediately
Implement OPA/Gatekeeper policy to prevent cluster-admin bindings for workload SAs
Enable Kubernetes Audit logging for all secret access
kubectl_commands: kubectl create secret generic postgres-credentials --from-literal=password=$(openssl rand -base64 32) -n data --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.subjects[]?.name=="auth-sa")'
rca.summary: auth-sa ServiceAccount was over-privileged with cluster-admin binding, enabling a compromised auth pod to access secrets outside its scope. Full RBAC privilege escalation path identified with 95/100 risk score.
